title: How to Help Someone in Kundalini Crisis: A Guide for Friends and Family

body:
How to Help Someone in Kundalini Crisis: A Guide for Friends and Family

You're here because someone you care about is going through something terrifying. Maybe they're talking crazy, maybe they're writhing in pain, maybe they're just not themselves. And you, you're scared. You're confused. You feel helpless. I get it. I've been there, both as the one going through it and as the one watching someone else. This isn't some New Age fluff; it's a real, raw, sometimes brutal process. And you're not alone in trying to figure it out.

Look, kundalini crisis - or spiritual emergency, whatever you wanna call it - it's not in the DSM-5. Not really. Doctors, therapists, they often don't know what the hell to do with it. They might label it psychosis, bipolar, anxiety, depression. And sometimes, yeah, it looks like that. But it's different. It's a deep, involuntary awakening of energy, consciousness, whatever you wanna call it, that can rip you apart before it puts you back together. Or, sometimes, it just rips you apart. That's why you're here. You need to know what you're dealing with, and how to actually help, not just stand by feeling useless.

What Even IS This Thing?

So, what are we even talking about? Imagine a dam breaking inside someone. All this energy, this power, that's usually dormant, it just erupts. It can be physical - intense heat or cold, tremors, involuntary movements, weird sensations, chronic pain that doctors can't explain. It can be mental - racing thoughts, paranoia, delusions, visions, hearing things, feeling like you're losing your mind. And emotional - extreme highs and lows, terror, bliss, deep despair, uncontrollable crying or laughing. It's a lot. It's too much, often. It's like their entire operating system is crashing and rebooting, all at once, without a manual.

I've seen people literally vibrate, you know? Like their whole body is a tuning fork. I've felt it myself. It's not a joke. It's not "making it up for attention." It's a real, physiological and psychological experience that can be incredibly disorienting and painful. And for the person going through it, they often feel utterly alone, misunderstood, and like they're going insane. They probably think they're dying, or possessed, or both. And you, you're probably thinking the same thing, just trying not to say it out loud.

It's Not Always "Good"

Here's the thing: a lot of spiritual communities talk about kundalini like it's this amazing, blissful experience. And yeah, for some, it is. But for others, it's a nightmare. It's not something you choose, not always. Sometimes it just hits you, out of nowhere, or after a period of intense stress, trauma, spiritual practice, or even just a major life change. It's like a spiritual tsunami. You don't ask for it, you just get swept away. And when you're caught in that current, you need someone to throw you a lifeline, not just tell you to "enjoy the ride." Right?

You might hear them talking about past lives, aliens, angels, demons, conspiracies, or feeling like they're connected to everything or nothing. It's confusing. It's scary. And your first instinct might be to rationalize it, to tell them they're wrong, to try and bring them back to "reality." Don't. Not yet, anyway. That's not helpful. That just makes them feel more isolated. They're already questioning their own reality. What they need is grounding, not more questioning.

First, Stabilize and Ground

Your primary role right now is to be a stable anchor in their storm. This isn't about fixing them; it's about holding space for them to be utterly broken without breaking yourself in the process. It's about safety. Physical safety, emotional safety. They're probably feeling unsafe in their own body, in their own mind. You need to be a place of calm.

  • Keep them safe: Are they a danger to themselves or others? This is the absolute first question. If you genuinely think they might harm themselves or someone else, you gotta get professional help - even if that means calling emergency services. I know it's hard, it feels like a betrayal, but their life, or someone else's, could depend on it. Don't play hero if it's truly beyond your ability to keep them safe.
  • Normalize, but don't validate delusions: This is a fine line. You can say, "I can see you're really scared right now," or "I hear you saying you feel like bugs are crawling under your skin, that sounds really distressing." You're acknowledging their experience, their feelings. You're not saying, "Yes, there are bugs under your skin." You're giving their feelings validity, not their misperceptions. Know what I mean?
  • Focus on the physical: When someone's mind is going haywire, getting them back into their body can be a lifesaver.
    • Food: Simple, grounding foods. Soups, stews, root vegetables. Nothing too stimulating. No caffeine, no sugar rushes. Just good, solid nourishment. They might not want to eat, but try to get them to. Even small amounts.
    • Water: Dehydration makes everything worse. Keep water nearby, remind them to drink.
    • Sleep: This is huge. Kundalini can seriously mess with sleep cycles. They might be wired for days. Lack of sleep fuels paranoia and psychosis. If they're not sleeping at all, you might need to talk to a doctor about temporary, gentle sleep aids. This isn't about medicating away the experience, but about preventing a complete breakdown.
    • Warmth and Comfort: Blankets, warm baths, soft clothes. Make their physical environment as soothing as possible.
    • Movement: Gentle walks, stretching, anything to get them moving their body without overstimulating it. Not intense exercise. Just enough to feel their feet on the ground.
  • Routine: Even a loose routine can help. Regular mealtimes, a consistent bedtime (even if they can't sleep), simple daily tasks. Structure creates a sense of predictability when their inner world is chaos.

Listen, Don't Judge

This is probably the hardest part for most people. You're gonna hear some wild stuff. They might be convinced they're a prophet, or that the government is watching them, or that they're communicating with spirits. Your gut reaction might be to argue, to correct, to explain why they're wrong. Don't. Seriously, don't.

  • Listen actively: Let them talk. Just listen. Don't interrupt. Don't try to solve it. Just be present. Sometimes, just having someone hear them, without judgment, is the most healing thing. It tells them they're not completely alone in their madness.
  • Validate their feelings, not their beliefs: Again, that fine line. "I can see how terrifying that must be," or "It sounds like you're feeling incredibly powerful right now." You're reflecting their emotional state, not agreeing that they're Jesus.
  • Avoid "shoulds": Don't tell them they "shouldn't feel that way" or "should just snap out of it." They can't. If they could, they would.
  • Be patient: This isn't a quick fix. This can last weeks, months, sometimes even years, in varying degrees of intensity. You're in for the long haul. Your patience is a gift.

Set Boundaries, Protect Yourself

You can't pour from an empty cup. This is draining work. You'll get frustrated, scared, angry, exhausted. That's okay. Acknowledge it. And then do something about it.

  • Don't get sucked into their delusions: It's easy to start questioning your own reality when someone you love is so convinced of theirs. Remember your own grounding. Stick to what you know is real for you.
  • Take breaks: You need time away. Get out, see other people, do things you enjoy. You're not abandoning them; you're recharging so you can actually help them.
  • Seek your own support: Talk to a trusted friend, a therapist, a spiritual advisor - someone who can handle what you're going through without judging you or the person in crisis. You need an outlet.
  • Don't be afraid to say "no": If they're making demands that are unreasonable, unsafe, or beyond your capacity, it's okay to say no. You're not responsible for their experience, only for how you respond to it.

Professional Help - The Tricky Part

This is where it gets really complicated because, as I said, most mainstream professionals don't get this. But sometimes, you absolutely need outside help.

When to Seek Medical/Psychiatric Help

Look, if they're severely psychotic, violent, completely catatonic, or suicidal, you don't have a choice. Get them to an emergency room or call 911. They need stabilization. They might need medication to bring them down from a severe episode. This isn't about stopping their spiritual process; it's about preventing permanent harm or death. Sometimes, temporary medication can provide the breathing room they need for the energy to settle a bit, to regain some coherence. It's a tool, not a cure.

If you can find a psychiatrist or therapist who is open-minded about spiritual experiences, who won't immediately pathologize everything, that's gold. They're rare, but they exist. Ask around in spiritual communities, even if you're not part of one yourself. Sometimes, "transpersonal" psychologists are more open to this stuff.

Alternative Support and Resources

There are a few resources out there that might help you understand what's happening, and maybe even find someone who gets it. They're not perfect, but they're a starting point.

  • Books: Reading up can help you feel less alone and more informed. I'll tell you what, The Kundalini Guide by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link) is a classic. She went through it herself and dedicated her life to helping others. Another really important one is Spiritual Emergency: When Personal Transformation Becomes a Crisis (paid link). That's a collection of essays from various experts, and it really broadens the perspective beyond just kundalini. For a more direct, personal account, The Kundalini Experience by Lee Sannella (paid link) can be helpful. And if you're looking for practical guidance for finding help, A Sourcebook for Helping People with Spiritual Problems by Emma Bragdon (paid link) offers some solid advice.
  • Spiritual counselors/coaches: Again, choose carefully. There are a lot of charlatans out there. Look for someone who emphasizes grounding, safety, and integration, not just "more awakening." Someone who has been through a crisis themselves, and come out the other side, can often offer incredible insight and empathy.
  • Support groups: Online forums or local groups (if you can find them) for spiritual emergence can be a godsend. Just knowing you're not the only one dealing with this crazy stuff can make a huge difference.

The Long Haul - Integration, Not Eradication

This isn't something you "cure." It's something you integrate. The goal isn't to stop the energy, but to help the person learn to live with it, to use it, to make sense of it, and to build a new life around it. It's a restructuring of their entire being. And that takes time. A lot of time. And a lot of patience.

  • Encourage creativity: Sometimes, expressing what's happening through art, writing, music, or dance can be incredibly cathartic and help them process.
  • Nature: Being in nature - just sitting in a park, walking by a river - can be incredibly grounding and soothing for an overstimulated nervous system.
  • Simple spiritual practices (if appropriate): If they're drawn to it, gentle, grounding practices like slow breathing, very simple meditation (not anything that ramps up energy), or quiet contemplation can be helpful. But be careful not to encourage anything that might intensify the experience further.
  • Remind them of who they are (beyond the crisis): Gently remind them of their strengths, their passions, their humor, their relationships, what they loved before this started. It helps them feel like they're not completely lost to this experience.

So yeah, it's a hell of a ride. For them, and for you. But your presence, your unwavering belief in their worth, even when they're at their most chaotic, that's what matters. You're not just a caretaker; you're a witness. And sometimes, being truly witnessed is the first step back to sanity. It's tough, it's messy, and you'll doubt yourself a thousand times. But here's the truth is, you're doing something truly courageous by standing by them. You're giving them a chance to come through this. Does that land? I hope so. Just keep breathing. Both of you.

This is not a substitute for professional mental health care. If you're in crisis, please reach out to a qualified therapist or call 988.
author: Kalesh
